Issue 042 April 14, 2026 8 min read

The real cost is always hidden until it isn't.

I spent 30,000 hours in the darkness and the bill came due in ways I didn't see coming. This issue is about the cost you can't calculate until you've paid it.

M
Michael Fjell
Founder · still doing the work

There is a specific kind of arithmetic that only makes sense in retrospect. You don't see it while you're spending. You don't see it for the first five years, or the first ten. You see it on a Tuesday afternoon, standing in your kitchen, when you realize you can't remember the last time you felt something all the way through.

That was the bill for me. I added it up a long time later and the number was 30,000 hours. Not a dramatic number — roughly three and a half years of continuous time, spread across two decades. The drama isn't in the hours. It's in what got displaced.

"You have to go inside your inner dungeon and scrub every shelf, every single day."

What you pay with

Money is the least of it. Money is visible — you can look at a bank balance and know where it went. The real currencies here are darker: attention, capacity, and the specific kind of quiet confidence that makes a man feel like he lives in his own life.

Attention is first because it's the most measurable in hindsight. Every minute spent in that loop is a minute not spent building something. Not a book, not a muscle, not a conversation. The compound interest of ten years of missing minutes is a person who arrives at 35 having practiced mostly one thing.

Capacity is the second one, and it's subtler. The brain is not a hydraulic system where you pump desire out one hole and it refills the next day. It's more like a muscle that learns the shape of what you ask of it. Ask it for intensity from a screen for twenty years and it will have a hard time finding intensity anywhere else. The wiring is just wiring — but it's your wiring, and you live in it.

The hidden one

The third cost is the one I didn't see coming, and it's the reason I'm writing this newsletter instead of a better version of my old life. It's presence. The ability to be in the room you're actually in.

There is a man at my dinner table who looks like me and says the right things at the right times. For most of my twenties and half of my thirties, that man was not me. I was somewhere else, usually in a loop, half-present to everything. My wife knew before I did. Kids know before you do. You're the last one to notice you've left the room.

That one can't be bought back. You can build new rooms and be fully in them — I do, every day now — but the rooms that are gone are gone.

Why this keeps working

I've now spent four years outside the loop and I can tell you the pattern: the cost compounds silently, and then one day it stops being silent. For some men that's a health thing, for some it's a marriage thing, for some it's the moment they catch themselves lying to a kid about nothing. The bill doesn't care how it arrives. It just arrives.

The point of this newsletter, and the community, and the tracker, is to move the arrival forward. To let you see the cost before it's been fully paid. Every man I know in recovery wishes he had seen it earlier. Nobody says the opposite.

Next week I'll write about what starts to grow back once the bleeding stops. It's the part that nobody tells you about, and it's the only reason any of this is worth doing.

— Michael
